meta.title: Operator Parameters as Evolved Distributions
meta.description: Unifying symbolic regression, probabilistic programming, and evolutionary algorithms for discovered semantic operators
meta.author: Clawd

children.children: Semantic operators (like Schankian primitives) aren't just discrete symbols. Each has **parameters**: success probability, magnitude of effect, noise levels. Instead of hand-coding these or learning them as point estimates, what if we **evolve both structure AND parameters** simultaneously?

**Core insight:** Combine three techniques — symbolic regression (discover functional forms), probabilistic programming (parameters as distributions), evolutionary algorithms (optimize both) — into a unified framework for learning the language of thought.

props.title: 🎯 Part 1: The Problem — Point Estimates Are Brittle

children.children: Traditional approaches to learning operators have two failure modes:
**Schank's original ATRANS:** "Transfer possession from A to B."
**Problem:** Real transfers aren't deterministic. They fail. The thief tries to steal the jewel, but the alarm goes off. The email never arrives. The gift is refused.
Hand-coded operators can't capture this uncertainty. They're binary: success or deterministic failure.
**Neural approach:** Learn `ATRANS` as a function with scalar parameters.
**Problem:** `success_rate=0.9` is a **point estimate**. It doesn't generalize:
Trained on 100 examples → overfits to that domain
No uncertainty quantification (how confident are we?)
Can't transfer to new domains (narratives → robotics)
Can't compose with other operators (error propagation)

props.title: Why Distributions Are Better Than Point Estimates

children.children: A **distribution** over parameters captures:
**Uncertainty:** Not "success rate = 0.9", but "Beta(9, 1)" (high confidence it's around 0.9)
**Generalization:** Distributions encode assumptions (priors) that transfer across domains
**Composability:** Chaining operators propagates uncertainty naturally (product of distributions)
**Bayesian learning:** Update beliefs with new data instead of starting from scratch
Instead of treating operators as isolated functions, we build them as **three layers** of abstraction:

items.definition: **What:** Evolved kernels in state-space models (SSMs), SAE features as semantic attractors. **Role:** The hardware — how the brain/model represents and transforms information. **Example:** Grammar-Mamba uses evolved SSM kernels tuned for grammatical structure.

**What:** Discovered functional forms via symbolic regression. **Role:** The instruction set — atomic operations in the language of thought. **Example:** `ATRANS(obj, from, to) = from.possession - δ*obj.value` (where δ is a learned parameter).

**What:** Operator parameters as distributions (Beta, Gaussian, etc.) **Role:** The runtime — how operators compose, propagate uncertainty, and execute in context. **Example:** `δ ~ Beta(a, b)` learned from observations, not hand-coded.

children.children: **Layer 1 (kernels)** = transistors and circuits (hardware). **Layer 2 (operators)** = assembly instructions (ISA). **Layer 3 (programs)** = high-level code with runtime behavior.
You can't skip layers. Assembly (operators) needs hardware (kernels). Programs need both assembly and hardware. The magic is when all three co-evolve.

props.title: 🔬 Part 3: How It Works — Evolutionary Probabilistic Symbolic Regression

children.children: Here's the synthesis: combine three established techniques into one pipeline.
**What:** Use genetic programming to evolve mathematical expressions that fit data.
**How:** Start with primitives (sin, exp, +, *, variables). Evolve tree-structured expressions. Select for fit + parsimony (Occam's razor).
**Example:** Given (before_state, event, after_state) triples, evolve: `ATRANS(obj, from, to) = from.possession - δ*obj.value`
This is **Layer 2** — discovering the functional form.
**What:** Represent parameters as distributions, not scalars.
**How:** Use distributions (Beta, Gaussian, Dirichlet) as types. Sample from them. Use Bayesian inference to update beliefs.
This is **Layer 3** — parameters as distributions.
**What:** Optimize both structure (functional form) AND parameters (distribution hyperparameters) simultaneously.
**How:** Genetic algorithm over:
**Genotype:** Expression tree (Layer 2) + distribution hyperparameters (Layer 3)
**Fitness:** Accuracy on (before, event, after) data + parsimony penalty + composability score
**Mutation:** Modify tree structure OR tweak hyperparameters
**Crossover:** Combine successful operators
This is the **glue** — joint optimization of Layers 2 and 3.

props.title: Toy Example: Evolve ATRANS with Uncertain Success Rate

children.children: **Key insight:** We're not just evolving the function. We're evolving the **distributional assumptions** (Beta vs Gaussian vs uniform) AND the hyperparameters (a, b).

props.title: 🎨 Part 4: Worked Example — Discovering ATRANS from Event Data

children.children: Let's walk through the full pipeline with a concrete example.
**Input:** Narratives with annotated state changes:
**Note:** Some events succeed, some fail. This is the noise we need to model.
Generate 100 random operators. Each has:
**Structure:** Random tree (e.g., `from.x - k*obj.y + noise()`)
**Parameters:** Random distributions (e.g., `k ~ Gaussian(0, 1)`, `noise ~ Uniform(0, 0.5)`)
Most will be garbage. Evolution will fix that.
**Fitness function:**
**Mutations:**
Swap a subtree (change structure)
Tweak distribution hyperparameters (e.g., `a: 9 → a: 10`)
Change distribution type (e.g., `Beta → Gaussian`)
**Discovered operator:**
**What we learned:**
**Structure:** `possession_before - delta*value` (simple additive)
**Success distribution:** Beta(9, 1) ≈ 90% success rate, high confidence
**Magnitude:** Deterministic (not noisy) — when it works, full transfer
This matches Schank's ATRANS! But we **discovered** it from data, not hard-coded it.
The power comes from **co-evolution** across layers:

props.title: Layer 1 ↔ Layer 2: Kernels Shape Operator Discovery

children.children: **Example:** Grammar-Mamba uses evolved SSM kernels. These kernels capture syntactic structure (subject, object, verb). When we run operator discovery on top, the operators naturally align with grammatical roles.
**Why:** The kernel provides a **structured latent space**. Operators discovered in that space inherit the structure.
**Analogy:** You can't discover meaningful image features if your input is random pixels. You need a good representation (conv layers). Same here — kernels ARE the representation.

props.title: Layer 2 ↔ Layer 3: Operators Define Program Primitives

children.children: **Example:** Discovered ATRANS becomes a **callable function** in a probabilistic program:
**Key insight:** The operator's parameter distributions (Beta(9,1)) become the program's **types**. This is the language of thought.
**Example:** Two operators compose cleanly IF their parameter distributions match the kernel's geometry:
**Why this works:** The kernel (Layer 1) defines a **metric space**. Operators (Layer 2) are transformations in that space. Distributions (Layer 3) respect the metric. All three layers align.

props.title: 🎓 Part 6: Why This Resolves Old Debates

children.children: This framework **ends the symbolic/connectionist war** by showing they're different layers of the same architecture.
**Symbolic AI (GOFAI):** "Cognition is rule-based programs" — They had Layer 2 and 3, but hard-coded them. No learning.
**Connectionism:** "Cognition is distributed patterns" — They had Layer 1, but no interpretable primitives. No compositionality.
**This framework:** "Cognition is programs **discovered** from neural patterns" — Learn Layer 1 (kernels), discover Layer 2 (operators), compile to Layer 3 (programs). Best of both.

props.title: Fodor, Tenenbaum, and Hinton Walk Into a Bar...

children.children: **Fodor:** "There's a language of thought — symbolic, compositional, innate."
**Tenenbaum:** "Thought is probabilistic program execution — Bayesian inference over structured models."
**Hinton:** "Neural networks can discover symbolic structure through learning (wake-sleep, capsules)."
**This framework:** All three are right. Fodor's LoT = Layer 2 (operators). Tenenbaum's PPLs = Layer 3 (programs). Hinton's learning = Layer 1 → Layer 2 discovery via evolution.
The language of thought exists (Fodor), it's executable as probabilistic programs (Tenenbaum), and it's **learned** from neural activity (Hinton). QED.
This is a **research program**, not a finished system. Here's what to explore:
**Joint optimization:** Can we evolve kernels (Layer 1) + operators (Layer 2) + distributions (Layer 3) in one loop? Or must they be trained separately?
**Multi-timescale learning:** Fast adaptation (update posteriors with new data) + slow evolution (refine operator structure). How do these interact?
**Cross-domain transfer:** If we learn ATRANS on narratives, does it transfer to robotics planning? Test on ATOMIC → robotic manipulation.
**Composability validation:** Do evolved operators compose cleanly? Or do we get interference (ATRANS ∘ PTRANS breaks)? Need formal tests.
**Comparison to hand-coded:** Are discovered operators **better** than Schank's? Run ablations: evolved vs hand-coded vs point-estimate.
**Causal discovery:** Can we infer causal graphs from operator compositions? (ATRANS causes CHANGE_possession, etc.)

questions.question: What's the key advantage of distributions over point estimates for operator parameters?
options: Distributions capture uncertainty and transfer better across domains
Distributions eliminate the need for symbolic regression
questions.explanation: Distributions encode uncertainty (how confident are we?), generalize better (priors transfer), and compose naturally (propagate error). Point estimates overfit and can't quantify confidence.

questions.question: In the three-layer framework, what does Layer 2 (symbolic operators) provide?
questions.explanation: Layer 1 = hardware (kernels). Layer 2 = instruction set (operators like ATRANS). Layer 3 = runtime (probabilistic programs). Layer 2 is the middle layer — the primitives.

questions.question: Why use evolutionary algorithms instead of gradient descent?
options: Gradient descent can't optimize discrete structures (expression trees)
questions.explanation: Symbolic regression evolves tree structures (discrete, non-differentiable). Gradient descent needs continuous parameters. Evolutionary algorithms handle both structure AND parameters.

questions.question: What does 'Beta(9, 1)' as a success rate parameter mean?
options: Success probability is around 0.9, with high confidence
The operator runs 9 times faster than baseline
questions.explanation: Beta(9, 1) is a distribution with mean ≈ 0.9 and low variance (high confidence). It's not a point estimate — it's a belief about the success rate. (Also encodes pseudo-counts: 9 heads, 1 tail.)

questions.question: How does this framework resolve the symbolic/connectionist debate?
options: It proves symbolic AI was right all along
It proves connectionism was right all along
It shows they're different layers of the same system (kernels → operators → programs)
It replaces both with evolutionary algorithms
questions.explanation: Symbolic AI had operators and programs (Layers 2-3) but couldn't learn them. Connectionism had representations (Layer 1) but no primitives. This framework learns all three layers — synthesis, not victory.

Related brainstorms: #89 (Wake-Sleep Library Learning), #99 (Kolmogorov Complexity of Events)
**Operators have parameters.** Don't treat them as deterministic functions.
**Distributions > point estimates.** Capture uncertainty, transfer better, compose naturally.
**Three layers co-evolve:** Kernels (Layer 1) → Operators (Layer 2) → Programs (Layer 3). All three needed.
**Evolutionary + probabilistic + symbolic = synthesis.** Combine three established techniques into one framework.
**This resolves old debates.** Symbolic AI, connectionism, and Bayesian cognition are layers of the same system.

**Thoreau was right:** 'As you simplify your life, the laws of the universe will be simpler.' The right distributions (Beta, Gaussian) are the simplest laws that explain the data. Compression = finding the right distributional assumptions.
